chunk_summary: Short bowel syndrome management involves staged feeding phases: acute, adaptation, and maintenance, with early parenteral nutrition (PN) and gradual transition to enteral feeds to support growth and intestinal adaptation.
learning_objectives: Understand the three feeding phases in short bowel syndrome: acute, adaptation, and maintenance.
Identify the role of parenteral nutrition (PN) and its gradual reduction during intestinal adaptation.
Recognize the importance of breast milk and formula selection in pediatric short bowel syndrome.
Learn prognostic indicators based on residual bowel length in term and preterm infants.
key_facts: Short bowel syndrome requires staged feeding: acute, adaptation, and maintenance phases.
Early PN is critical in the acute phase to meet fluid and energy needs and prevent growth failure.
Residual bowel length >15 cm with an ileocecal valve or >40 cm without is associated with better outcomes in term infants.
Breast milk is preferred for infants; if unavailable, whole-protein or hydrolyzed formulas are alternatives.
Early postoperative enteral feeding supports faster progression to full oral feeds and intestinal adaptation.
reference_quote: Management is typically described in phases: acute phase, adaptation phase, and maintenance phase. Early postoperative enteral feeding is associated with faster progression to full oral feeds in some evidence and supports intestinal adaptation.
